close_reason: Closed
description: In crates/ttrpg_interp/src/adapter.rs, clamp_value() uses Rust clamp directly for Int/Float (lines 503-507). i64::clamp/f64::clamp panic when lo > hi, and f64::clamp also panics when either bound is NaN. compute_field_value() calls clamp_value() for bounded mutations (line 390), so malformed or unexpected bounds values can crash the process instead of returning a RuntimeError. This is a production stability bug.
title: clamp_value can panic on invalid bounds and crash interpreter

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_control.rs

Why this is a bug: check_expr_expecting carries expected-type hints to disambiguate variants and infer literals, but ExprKind::If and ExprKind::IfLet call into check_if/check_if_let which currently type-check branches via check_block/check_expr without threading any hint. This drops contextual type information for branch bodies. As a result, valid code can fail with ambiguity/type errors (for example, bare variants that require an expected enum hint, or branch literals that rely on expected element types).

Potential impact: Valid if/if let expressions in typed contexts (return positions, argument positions, etc.) can be rejected with false type errors, while equivalent match expressions type-check correctly because they do thread hints. This is a user-visible type-checking inconsistency.

Suggested fix: Add an optional expected-type hint parameter to check_if/check_if_let and check_else_branch_type, then check then/else blocks with check_block_with_tail_hint(..., hint) and nested else-if expressions with check_expr_expecting(..., hint) so branch inference/disambiguation uses context.
title: Bug: if/if let branches ignore expected type hints

close_reason: pipe/script mode now continues on error instead of exiting immediately, so diagnostics are reachable
description:
In pipe mode (run_pipe, main.rs:38) and script mode (run_script, main.rs:64), any command error calls process::exit(1) immediately. This means if `load` fails with parse/check errors, the subsequent `errors` command never runs — the user cannot see what went wrong.

The diagnostics ARE stored on the Runner (runner.rs:280), but the process exits before the next line of stdin/script is read.

Options:
- Don't exit on load failure in pipe/script mode — just print the error and continue
- Auto-print diagnostics on load failure (first N errors)
- Add a --keep-going flag for script mode
- In pipe mode, treat load failure as non-fatal (the Runner is still usable, just with no program loaded)
title: CLI: pipe/script mode exits on load failure, diagnostics unreachable

description: File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_pattern.rs. In PatternKind::Ident (lines 108-110) and PatternKind::BareDestructure (lines 289-291), owners.len() == 1 immediately resolves the variant owner without checking that scrutinee_ty is Ty::Enum of that owner. close_reason: Added missing check_name_visible calls for method-style action calls and emit statements
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_call.rs

Why this is a bug: In check_call, the ExprKind::FieldAccess path treats entity-dot-action calls as action calls by looking up the field in env.lookup_fn and returning early, but it never calls check_name_visible for the action name. Direct calls do enforce visibility via check_name_visible with Namespace::Function. This creates inconsistent semantics and allows calling non-visible actions via method syntax.

Potential impact: Code that should fail module visibility/import checks can type-check successfully when rewritten as entity.action(...), bypassing encapsulation boundaries and incorrectly accepting invalid programs.

Suggested fix: In the method-style action branch, run the same non-builtin visibility check as direct calls (for example, check_name_visible(field, Namespace::Function, span)) before validating arguments and return type.
title: Bug: Method-style action calls bypass visibility checks

description: In crates/ttrpg_checker/src/collect.rs:58-62, collect_inner calls pass_1b separately for each system. Inside pass_1b (crates/ttrpg_checker/src/collect.rs:252), processed_types is local to one call. If two systems declare the same type name, pass_1a reports a duplicate (lines 228-239) and keeps the first stub, but the second system declaration is still processed in a later pass_1b call and writes into the same existing entry via get_mut in collectors (for example lines 431-433, 458-460, 541-544, 619-622). Impact: a duplicate declaration that should be ignored can overwrite the canonical type fields or variants, causing inconsistent downstream type checking.

close_reason: Fixed all 4 alias/shadow resolution bugs
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_has.rs

Why this is a bug: In check_has(), alias shadow validation only checks env.lookup_fields(entity) and env.lookup_optional_group(entity, alias). For entities with required include groups, flattened fields are exposed via env.lookup_flattened_field(entity, field) during field resolution, but check_has does not guard against alias collisions with those flattened names. As a result, a valid field like m.ac (from an included group) can be silently reinterpreted as an alias to a different optional group inside a has-guarded scope.

Potential impact: Incorrect name resolution and type checking in valid programs. Accesses to flattened included fields can change meaning or fail unexpectedly inside if entity has Group as alias blocks, producing misleading diagnostics and incorrect behavior for group-aware logic.

Suggested fix: In check_has alias validation for Ty::Entity, also check env.lookup_flattened_field(entity_name, alias_name) and emit the same shadowing error when present.
title: Bug: has-alias shadow check misses flattened included fields

Repro shape: append(list<DamageType>, fire) where fire is shared by multiple enums should use DamageType as hint but currently reports ambiguity.

close_reason: Migrated dnd5e_expanded to use map<int, resource(0..9)> for spell_slots. Replaced 9 spell_slots_N fields with single resource-valued map. Replaced 9-branch matches in CureWounds and Fireball with spell_slots[slot_level] -= 1. Updated pain point comments and summary. File loads and type-checks cleanly.
description:
Spell slots in 5e are a resource indexed by level (1-9). The DSL forces 9 separate fields:
    spell_slots_1: int = 0
    spell_slots_2: int = 0
    ...
    spell_slots_9: int = 0

Ideally: `spell_slots: map<int, resource(0..max_slots)>`
But resource types cannot be values in a map. There is also no way to use a runtime int to select a field (no computed field access like `entity.spell_slots[level]`).
title: Language: indexed resources / map of resources

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_ast/src/span.rs

Why this is a bug: Span::merge relies on debug_assert_eq!(self.file.0, other.file.0), which is compiled out in release builds. When called with spans from different files, it silently returns a merged span that keeps self.file but combines start/end from both spans, producing an invalid source location.

Potential impact: Incorrect source mapping in diagnostics or downstream logic, with hard-to-trace misattribution of errors to the wrong file.

Suggested fix: Enforce same-file merging in all builds (e.g., assert!/panic, Result-returning API, or explicit cross-file handling) instead of debug_assert-only validation.
title: Bug: Span::merge allows cross-file merge in release builds

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check.rs

Why this is a bug: check_condition and check_event type-check parameter defaults inside a fresh scope but never bind previously declared parameters before checking later defaults. This differs from bind_params (used for functions/actions/prompts), which binds params incrementally so defaults like b: int = a are valid. As written, valid condition/event defaults that reference earlier params are incorrectly reported as undefined/type errors.

Potential impact: Valid DSL programs are rejected during checking when condition/event defaults depend on prior parameters, despite runtime argument binding supporting this pattern.

Suggested fix: Reuse bind_params-style incremental binding for condition/event default validation (or explicitly bind prior params in order while checking defaults), so earlier parameters are in scope for later default expressions.
title: Bug: Condition/event default params cannot reference earlier params

close_reason: Fixed with regression tests
description: In crates/ttrpg_checker/src/scope.rs lines 181-188, is_group_narrowed scans narrowed_groups across all active scopes and returns true on any matching key, but it does not account for identifier shadowing. If an outer binding/path like actor is narrowed, then an inner scope binds a new actor, checks for the inner actor can incorrectly inherit the outer proof. This can allow optional-group field access or with-constrained calls to type-check without a valid has/with proof for the current binding. Suggested fix: stop lookup when a nearer scope binds the root identifier, or track narrowings by binding identity instead of raw string keys.
title: ScopeStack::is_group_narrowed leaks optional-group proofs across shadowed bindings

description:
File: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/effects.rs

Why this is a bug: roll_dice normalizes sides with (group.sides as i64).max(1), so d0 is treated as 1..=1. roll_dice_from_queue validates queued values against 1..=group.sides directly, so with sides == 0 it rejects all queued values and vetoes.

Potential impact: Queued deterministic rolls can fail while non-queued rolls succeed for the same expression, causing inconsistent runtime behavior.

Suggested fix: Normalize sides in roll_dice_from_queue the same way as roll_dice before validation and fallback RNG.
title: Bug: Queue dice validation diverges from normal d0 handling

description: In crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call.rs lines 660-672, dispatch_table_with_values forwards pre-evaluated args without validating arity. In dispatch_table_core at line 700, entry keys are compared with arg values using zip, which truncates to the shorter side. Extra args are ignored and missing args leave trailing keys unchecked, so an entry can be treated as a match when full-arity matching should fail. This can return incorrect table results instead of an arity error for pre-evaluated table calls.
title: dispatch_table_with_values can match wrong entries when arg count is wrong

This is a harmless convenience. Update the spec grammar to make braces optional: event_decl = "event" IDENT "(" params ")" ("{" field_defs "}")?.

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/collect.rs

Why this is a bug: In validate_option_extends(), cycle detection notices revisits via visited, but the error-chain construction assumes the cycle closes back to start_name. For a graph like A->B->C->B, starting from A detects a cycle at B; the chain builder then walks A->B->C->B->... and never reaches A, so the while-loop never terminates.

Potential impact: The checker can hang indefinitely on invalid option-extends input, blocking diagnostics and potentially wedging CLI/editor workflows.

Suggested fix: When building the cycle message, stop when revisiting any node (or when revisiting the detected cycle node) instead of requiring parent == start_name; alternatively track predecessor/path during detection and reconstruct only the actual cycle segment.
title: Bug: Option extends cycle error builder can loop forever

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/pattern.rs

Why this is a bug: In parse_pattern, the raw_name == 'some' recovery branch runs before qualified-pattern handling. Inputs like some.Variant or some.Variant(x) are valid qualified patterns when a type or alias is named some, but they are misparsed as bare some and converted to PatternKind::Some(_) with a diagnostic.

Potential impact: Valid match, if-let, and for patterns become unparsable or are parsed incorrectly when the leading type or alias identifier is some, causing false errors and broken behavior in affected code.

Suggested fix: Only apply bare-some recovery when the next token is not Dot; allow the existing qualified-pattern path (IDENT.DOT.IDENT...) to run first for some.* forms.
title: Bug: Pattern parser rejects qualified names starting with 'some'

description: In crates/ttrpg_parser/src/parser.rs, suppress_newlines_in_brace_block() (lines 149-175) scans forward to find a matching RBrace, but if no matching brace is found depth never reaches 0 and end becomes tokens.len(). The subsequent removal loop then deletes every Newline token from self.pos to EOF. This mutates the remaining token stream globally and causes downstream parse behavior to diverge (declarations/statements can be merged and recovery quality degrades sharply) after a single missing '}'. The function should bail out without mutation when no matching RBrace exists.
title: Brace newline suppression corrupts token stream when closing brace is missing

description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/builtins.rs

Why this is a bug: In builtin_remove_condition(), the ActiveCondition path reads id as Value::Int and converts it with a direct cast to u64. For negative ids this performs two's-complement wrapping, producing a huge positive u64 instead of rejecting invalid input.

Potential impact: A script can pass an ActiveCondition with id less than 0 and trigger RemoveCondition with an unintended large id, causing silent failure to remove the intended condition or incorrect host-side behavior if ids are interpreted directly.

Suggested fix: Validate id is non-negative before conversion and use checked conversion (for example u64::try_from(id)), returning RuntimeError on invalid values.
title: Bug: remove_condition wraps negative ActiveCondition id to u64

description: No way to automatically react when a resource crosses a boundary. Currently threshold checks are manual in every action that modifies HP (TakeDamage checks hp<=0). If HP is modified any other way (healing overflow, direct assignment, level drain), checks are missed. Two design options: (A) on-threshold syntax on entity resource fields: 'on hp <= 0 { ... }', or (B) auto-emit ResourceChanged event whenever interpreter mutates a resource, letting hooks react. Option B is lighter-weight and more composable. Blocks: auto-unconscious at 0 HP, auto-death at -10 HP, exhaustion thresholds, spell slot depletion triggers.
design:
## Design: restricted field modifier

### Summary
Add a `restricted` keyword modifier on entity/group fields that prevents mutation from outside the declaring module (system block). Read access is always allowed. This enforces encapsulation — resource mutations must go through actions exported by the declaring module.

### Syntax
```
entity Character {
    restricted HP: resource(0..=max_HP)
    restricted spell_slots: map<int, resource(0..=9)>
    name: string  // unrestricted, anyone can mutate
}

group HasHP {
    restricted hp: resource(0..=max_hp)
    max_hp: int
}
```

### Semantics
- `restricted` is opt-in per field (default: unrestricted)
- Applies to any entity or group field, not just resources
- **Declaring module** = the `system "Name" { ... }` block containing the field declaration
- Multiple `system "Name"` blocks with same name merge — all share write access
- Read access is always allowed from any module
- Assignment (`=`, `+=`, `-=`) to a restricted field is a **checker error** if the assignment statement is outside the declaring module

### Exceptions
- `grant entity.Group { field_inits }` is allowed cross-module (initialization, not ongoing mutation)
- `revoke entity.Group` is allowed cross-module (removes entire group, not field-level mutation)
- REPL direct assignment (`set`) bypasses restriction (debug tool)

### Module ownership for groups
If system A declares a group with a restricted field, and system B includes that group on an entity, **system A owns the restriction**. System B must call actions from system A to mutate the field. This is intentional: the group author controls the mutation contract.

### Error message
```
error: cannot mutate restricted field `HP` outside declaring module
  --> spells.tdsl:42:9
   |
42 |     target.HP = 0
   |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^ mutation not allowed here
   |
   = note: `HP` is declared as restricted in system "Core Combat"
   = help: use an action from "Core Combat" to modify this field
```

### Audit result
No cross-module mutations exist in the current codebase. This feature is forward-looking protection with zero migration burden.
title: Design: resource threshold triggers

Impact: user-declared types named Position or Condition are present in env.types but cannot be referenced by type annotations, causing unreachable declarations and confusing type mismatches.
title: Type resolver ignores user-defined Position/Condition declarations

close_reason: Closed
description:
In crates/ttrpg_parser/src/decl.rs, parse_with_groups (lines 294-314) consumes commas as group separators but only stops when lookahead is IDENT followed by Colon (lines 305-308). It does not stop on Comma followed by RParen.

When parsing a parameter like: derive f(actor: Character with Spellcasting,) -> int { ... }
parse_with_groups consumes the trailing comma at line 310, then calls expect_ident() at line 311 and fails on RParen.

This conflicts with parse_params trailing-comma support in the same file (parse_params lines 253-266, especially lines 257-260), so trailing commas work for normal params but fail when with constraints are present.

Impact: valid parameter lists with trailing commas become parse errors for declarations that use parse_params whenever a parameter uses with constraints.
title: Parser rejects trailing comma after parameter with-groups

close_reason: Refactored desugar_qualified_types to derive alias maps from validated module_map.systems[*].imports instead of rebuilding from raw file_systems use_decls
description: In resolve_modules(), Step 4 validates and deduplicates imports into module_map.systems[*].imports, but Step 6 (desugar_qualified_types) rebuilds alias maps from raw file_systems/use_decls. This can reintroduce invalid or conflicting aliases (for example duplicate aliases with different targets) and produce inconsistent follow-up diagnostics or wrong alias-to-system resolution during TypeExpr::Qualified desugaring. Refactor desugaring to derive aliases from validated imports in module_map (or otherwise skip invalid aliases captured during validation), so later phases consume the same canonical import state.
title: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/resolve.rs: desugaring uses raw file aliases instead of validated imports

close_reason: Design choice: defensive hardening only — no current code path creates malformed spans
description: In crates/ttrpg_ast/src/diagnostic.rs, span math is unchecked in render_diagnostic (lines 94-95 and 122). local_start = diag.span.start - base_offset / local_end = diag.span.end - base_offset and later local_end - local_start can underflow when a diagnostic has end < start (or when offsets are otherwise inconsistent). In debug builds this can panic; in release it can wrap to a huge usize and drive "^".repeat(caret_len) to attempt massive allocation, causing memory blowups/DoS. This is production-impacting because Diagnostic fields are public and spans are not validated at this boundary. Clamp/validate spans before arithmetic (e.g., saturating math + min/max against source bounds).
notes: Triage: debatable — unchecked usize subtraction exists but no current code path creates malformed spans. Defensive hardening only.
title: render_diagnostic can panic or OOM on invalid span ordering

close_reason: Implemented unit types across all compiler phases: lexer (UnitLiteral token), AST (UnitDecl + UnitLit), parser (unit decl + suffix literal), checker (Ty::UnitType, arithmetic/comparison rules), interpreter (eval + arithmetic ops), CLI (highlighter + types command)
description: Add unit types to the DSL as described in spec/v0/05_unit_types.ttrpg. A unit type wraps a single int field with type-safe arithmetic and optional literal suffix syntax (e.g., 30ft, 5gp). Declaration: unit TypeName suffix shortname { field: int }. Suffix is optional soft keyword. Lexer emits UnitLiteral tokens (digits+alpha, no space). Checker validates suffix maps to declared unit type. Arithmetic: same-type +/- returns unit, int*unit and unit*int scale, unit/unit returns float, unit/int and unit*unit are errors. Comparisons between same type produce bool, different types error. No implicit conversion to/from int.
title: Unit types: dimensioned numeric types with optional suffix literals

close_reason: Triaged: no measurable performance impact on realistic inputs. Small data sizes (3 outcomes, 5 options, 30 decls, 50 tokens) make algorithmic complexity irrelevant.
description: Ty::display constructs owned Strings for every call (including format! and clone()) and Display delegates through this allocation path. This is a code smell for a hot diagnostic/type-printing path. Refactor Display impl to write directly to fmt::Formatter (or expose a borrowed-display API) to reduce unnecessary allocations and cloning.
title: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/ty.rs: Avoid allocation-heavy Ty::display path

close_reason: Resource bounds now resolved from AST field declarations at mutation time. eval_assign_entity looks up entity type → field def → navigates TypeExpr along path to find resource bounds → evaluates bound expressions (literals + entity field refs). MutateField effects now carry actual bounds, and adapter clamping works for both direct resource fields and resource-valued map entries. 8 integration tests verify clamping for underflow, overflow, set, auto-init, dynamic bounds, and optional groups.
description: Extend the mutation/adapter path so that writing to a map<K, resource(min..max)> entry applies resource clamping. When the adapter handles MutateField with path [Field(f), Index(k)], look up the field declaration for f. If value type is resource(...), extract bound expressions, evaluate them in entity context, and clamp the result. This is the core new work — bounds lookup must unwrap the map value type to find the inner resource bounds.
title: Adapter: clamp writes to resource-valued map entries

close_reason: Closed
description: eval_add, eval_sub, and eval_comparison guard unit branches with as_unit_value checks, then recompute as_unit_value and unwrap the results. Even when currently safe, this pattern is brittle and duplicates work on runtime paths. Capture unit tuples once with if let or match binding and avoid unwrap in evaluator logic.
title: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval.rs: Remove guard plus recompute plus unwrap pattern in unit ops

close_reason: Fixed: check for RParen before parsing bindings, allowing empty binding lists
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/decl.rs

Why this is a bug: parse_trigger_param() always calls parse_trigger_bindings(), and parse_trigger_bindings() unconditionally parses at least one binding. This makes trigger clauses with empty binding lists unparseable. However, the AST/checker/runtime all support empty trigger binding vectors (matching by event name only), so parser behavior is inconsistent with the rest of the pipeline.

Potential impact: Reactions/hooks cannot target events without explicit bindings (including zero-parameter events), causing valid programs to fail at parse time.

Suggested fix: In parse_trigger_param(), mirror parse_modify_clause behavior: if the next token is RParen, use an empty vec; otherwise call parse_trigger_bindings().
title: Bug: Empty trigger binding list rejected

close_reason: Entity destruction is better modeled as conditions (Dead, Destroyed, Banished) rather than entity removal. TTRPGs almost universally keep entity data around for resurrection/repair. CLI 'destroy' command suffices for host-level cleanup.
description: No way to remove entities from the game. Death in 5e is permanent removal of a character, but the DSL has no destroy() or remove_entity() builtin. Entities are created via spawn but never destroyed. Needed for: character death, destroying objects, banishment effects.
title: Language: entity destruction / lifecycle management

close_reason: Fixed with regression tests enabled
description: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/lower.rs introduces a hard-coded local binding named result (line 227) and then reads that binding in generated guard arms (line 258). Because move receiver/params are user-controlled and not checked against this name during lowering, user code that uses a binding named result in outcome blocks gets captured by the synthetic binding. This can silently change behavior (for example comparisons now use roll result instead of the move argument) or produce confusing type errors. Lowering should generate a hygienic internal name or reject/conflict-check result in move bindings before synthesis.
title: Move lowering captures user result binding in synthesized action

In crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_stmt.rs, both check_grant and check_revoke state that these statements are only allowed in action or reaction context, but the gate uses scope.allows_mutation() (lines 290 and 389). Mutation permission includes hook context, so grant/revoke are accepted in hooks despite the stated rule and diagnostics text. This is a concrete checker policy mismatch.

File references:
- crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_stmt.rs:289-295
- crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_stmt.rs:388-394
title: grant/revoke context gate is inconsistent with stated action/reaction-only rule

close_reason: Closed
description:
The DSL has no loop construct. This blocks modeling:
- Extra Attack (make N attack rolls where N depends on level)
- AoE spells (apply damage to N targets)
- Multi-damage-type processing (iterate over list<DamageSpec>)
- Spell slot deduction (9-branch match instead of indexed access)

A bounded `for` loop would cover most cases:
    for target in targets { ... }
    for i in 1..attack_count(attacker) { ... }

Unbounded while loops are probably not needed for TTRPG modeling.
title: Language: bounded iteration / loops

close_reason: Already fixed in current codebase
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/state.rs

Why this is a bug: ActiveCondition::to_value stores id as Value::Int(self.id as i64). id is u64, so values above i64::MAX wrap/truncate on cast and become incorrect, even for valid host-provided IDs.

Potential impact: DSL-visible condition IDs can be corrupted, breaking comparisons, lookups, or diagnostics that rely on stable condition instance IDs.

Suggested fix: Preserve full u64 range by exposing id as a lossless representation (for example an unsigned value type or string), or guard and return an error on out-of-range values instead of lossy casting.
title: Bug: ActiveCondition id overflows when converted to Value::Int

close_reason: Resolved by design: explicit emit + mechanic wrappers (e.g. deal_damage) is the right pattern. Auto-emission from field mutations is too implicit and creates footguns (invisible cascades, infinite loops). Future privacy system will restrict direct field writes, making the mechanic-wrapper pattern compiler-enforced rather than convention-based.
notes: Partially addressed by emit statement (tdsl-wbic). DSL authors can now write `emit takes_damage(...)` alongside mutations. Remaining gap: mutations don't auto-emit — the emit must be written explicitly. Full auto-emission would require field-level on_decrease hooks.
title: Language: automatic event emission from mutations

title: Implement NPC reaction table

close_reason: Map literal syntax {key: value} already existed; fixed empty map type inference via tail-hint propagation, added map_lit to spec grammar, and added 8 integration tests
description: The spec grammar has no map_lit production in primary_expr. Maps can be typed (map<K, V>) and indexed (m[key]), but there's no way to construct a map literal in DSL code. Maps can only be populated via CLI 'set' commands. This means derives/mechanics that need to return or construct maps have no way to do so. Possible syntaxes: [key: value, ...] (Python-like, conflicts with list literal + colon) or {key: value} (conflicts with struct literal / block). Needs design work.
title: No map literal syntax in DSL expressions

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/action.rs

Why this is a bug: In collect_and_apply_cost_modifiers(), binding expressions are evaluated with eval_expr, but Err(_) is converted into all_match = false rather than being returned. This suppresses runtime expression errors during cost-modifier binding checks.

Potential impact: Valid programs can silently skip applicable cost modifiers when a binding expression hits a runtime error (for example division by zero), producing incorrect behavior and making failures hard to diagnose.

Suggested fix: Propagate eval_expr errors from binding evaluation (return Err) instead of treating them as a non-match, aligning behavior with modify binding checks in pipeline.rs.
title: Bug: Cost modify binding eval errors are silently ignored

description: Checker ignores arg.name and type-checks condition params by positional index only (check_expr.rs:819). Interpreter uses arg.name if present (call.rs:46), so Frightened(src: actor) passes checker but stores 'src' instead of 'source', causing runtime undefined variable. close_reason: Already fixed in bd4d2d1 — uses (expr.modifier as i128).unsigned_abs() with regression test
description: In crates/ttrpg_cli/src/format.rs lines 127-129, negative modifiers are rendered with expr.modifier.abs(). DiceExpr.modifier is i64, so i64::MIN is a valid runtime value. abs() on i64::MIN overflows: with overflow checks enabled this can panic; without checks it stays negative and produces malformed output like 1d20 - -9223372036854775808. This is user-visible CLI corruption and can crash debug builds. Suggested fix: use checked_abs()/unsigned_abs() and special-case i64::MIN so formatting never overflows.
title: format_dice_expr overflows/misformats i64::MIN modifier

close_reason: Closed
description:
Allow 'as <alias>' syntax at the two points where optional group existence is proven:

1. **`with` constraints**: `action CastSpell on caster: Character with AdvancedSpellcasting as sc (cost: int)` — alias scoped to the function body
2. **`has` guards**: `if caster has AdvancedSpellcasting as sc { caster.sc.spell_slots -= cost }` — alias scoped to the guard block

This lets users avoid repeating long group names in field access paths. The alias is always locally scoped (no global renaming), and only valid where narrowing has been proven.

Touches: parser (parse 'as ident' after group name in with/has), AST (store optional alias on WithConstraint and HasExpr), checker (register alias in scope for field resolution), interpreter (resolve alias during field access).
title: Add alias bindings for optional groups at narrowing sites

close_reason: Design choice: inherent brace ambiguity (map literal vs block) — same as Rust/Go/etc. Parenthesized workaround exists
description: Bug in crates/ttrpg_parser/src/expr.rs at parse_arm_body (lines 654-659). Arm bodies branch to parse_block whenever the next token is LBrace. Since map literals are expression forms starting with LBrace (parse_primary_expr lines 313-340), an arm like `k => {"x": 1}` is forced down block parsing and fails instead of parsing as ArmBody::Expr(MapLit). Repro: ttrpg_parser::parse_expr("match n { 1 => {\"x\": 1}, _ => {\"x\": 0} }") should parse, but currently errors unless each map literal is parenthesized.
title: match arm body cannot be map literal without extra parentheses

description: File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval.rs (collect_idents lines 2257-2291, eval_bound_expr lines 2299-2331, resolve_resource_bounds lines 2338-2359). eval_bound_expr retries bound evaluation by collecting identifiers and binding entity fields, but collect_idents does not traverse many expression variants (notably If then/else bodies, IfLet, PatternMatch, GuardMatch, StructLit, etc.). If a bound expression references entity fields inside one of those omitted subtrees, fallback misses required bindings, evaluation fails, and resolve_resource_bounds returns None. That silently drops bounds for MutateField, so clamping is skipped and out-of-range mutations can pass in production.
notes: Triage: real but low practical risk — collect_idents misses some expression forms but resource bounds are almost always simple expressions like 0..=max_HP.
title: resource bound fallback misses identifiers in many expression forms

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/runner/parse.rs

Why this is a bug: parse_spawn_block rejects duplicate field keys but does not reject duplicate inline group names. The returned groups vector can contain the same group multiple times, and cmd_spawn later writes each prepared group in order. This means a single spawn command can declare the same group twice and the last one silently wins, which is inconsistent with duplicate field handling and makes input order change semantics unexpectedly.

Potential impact: Users can accidentally provide conflicting values for the same optional group in one spawn block and get silently overwritten state instead of a validation error, leading to hard-to-debug incorrect character/entity setup.

Suggested fix: Track seen group names in parse_spawn_block (or before applying prepared groups) and return an explicit duplicate-group error when a name repeats, matching duplicate field behavior.
title: Bug: spawn allows duplicate inline groups with silent overwrite

description: Conditions are purely declarative today (modify + suppress). They cannot execute code when applied, removed, or each round. This blocks: bleeding at 0 HP (1 HP/round loss), poison over time, ongoing spell effects (Wall of Fire damage each round), condition side-effects (Unconscious should also apply Prone/drop items). Current workaround (orphan hooks on RoundStart that check for conditions) separates behavior from definition and only works inside resolve_round(). Proposed syntax: on_apply/on_tick/on_remove blocks inside condition declarations. Needs design doc covering: syntax, scoping rules (can on_tick mutate? emit?), tick trigger mechanism (who calls tick — interpreter or DSL event?), interaction with duration/expiry.
design:
# Condition Lifecycle Hooks: on_apply / on_remove (Phase 1)

> on_tick is deferred to a second design pass — it requires resolving
> game_time vs turn-relative timing semantics first.

## Motivation

Conditions are purely declarative today (modify + suppress). They cannot
execute imperative code when applied or removed. This blocks use cases like:
- Dropping held items when Unconscious is applied
- Breaking concentration when Stunned is applied
- Emitting status change events for UI/logging
- Cleaning up entity state when a condition wears off

Current workaround — orphan hooks that check `conditions(entity)` on
turn_start — separates behavior from its definition and requires the host
to fire the right events.

## Design Principle: extends over apply_condition

Conditions that imply other conditions should use `extends`, not
imperative `apply_condition()` inside on_apply:

```
// GOOD: declarative relationship
condition Unconscious extends Incapacitated, Prone on bearer: Character { ... }

// BAD: imperative sub-condition management
condition Unconscious on bearer: Character {
    on_apply { apply_condition(bearer, Prone, indefinite) }  // forbidden
}
```

`extends` gives automatic inheritance of modify/suppress clauses and
automatic cleanup on removal. No invocation ownership questions.

## Syntax

New `ConditionClause` variants alongside `Modify` and `Suppress`.
At most one `on_apply` and one `on_remove` per condition declaration.

```
condition Burning(damage: int) on bearer: Character {
    on_apply {
        emit StatusGained(target: bearer, status: "Burning")
        bearer.took_fire_damage = true
    }

    on_remove {
        emit StatusLost(target: bearer, status: "Burning")
    }

    modify fire_resistance(target: bearer) { ... }
    suppress regeneration(target: bearer)
}
```

## Trigger Points

**on_apply** fires immediately **before** the condition is activated.
The condition is not yet active — its modify/suppress clauses are NOT
in effect during on_apply execution. If on_apply succeeds, the condition
activates. If on_apply errors, the condition is never applied (no
rollback needed since it was never active).

**on_remove** fires immediately before the condition is stripped.
The condition's modify/suppress clauses are still in effect during
on_remove execution. Fires on:
- `remove_condition()` — explicit removal. When a single call matches
  multiple instances, on_remove fires **once per instance** in
  `gained_at` order (oldest first). All matched instances are removed
  regardless of individual on_remove errors; the first error is
  propagated after all removals complete.
- `revoke(invocation)` — bulk removal by invocation. The interpreter
  expands this into per-instance removals: each matching condition's
  on_remove fires individually, in `gained_at` order.
- Future auto-expiry (when on_tick is implemented)

## Capabilities

Lifecycle blocks have **hook-like** execution semantics:
- Can mutate entity state
- Can emit events
- Can roll dice
- Can call derives, mechanics, functions

## Restrictions (enforced)

The following are **directly forbidden** inside on_apply / on_remove blocks:
- `apply_condition()` — prevents reentrancy and condition graph antipattern
- `remove_condition()` — same reasoning
- `revoke(invocation)` — removes conditions, same restriction
- `invocation()` — no meaningful use since condition manipulation is
  forbidden; avoids ambiguity when caller has no active invocation

`revoke entity.Group` (optional group revocation) is **allowed** — that
manipulates entity structure, not conditions.

These restrictions are enforced at the checker level (static analysis)
for direct calls, with a runtime guard (`in_lifecycle_block` flag) as
fallback.

**Indirect bypass via emit**: a lifecycle block may emit an event whose
hook calls `apply_condition()`. This is **not transitively prevented** —
transitive enforcement would require tracing all reachable code paths
and would make `emit` unusable in lifecycle blocks. This is documented
as a discouraged pattern; the direct restriction catches the primary
antipattern (conditions managing condition graphs).

Rationale: if a use case arises that genuinely needs condition
manipulation inside lifecycle blocks, we can lift the restriction then
with a concrete motivating example to design around.

## Scoping

- `bearer` (the receiver) is in scope
- Condition parameters are in scope (e.g., `damage` for `Burning(damage: int)`)
- `invocation()` is **not available** (checker error)

## Inheritance via extends

If `condition Stunned extends Incapacitated on bearer: Character` and
both have lifecycle blocks:

- **on_apply**: ancestor blocks run first (DFS post-order, matching
  modify clause ordering). Incapacitated.on_apply then Stunned.on_apply.
  If an ancestor's on_apply errors, descendant on_apply blocks do not
  run and the condition is not applied.
- **on_remove**: same ancestor-first ordering.
  Incapacitated.on_remove then Stunned.on_remove.

This matches the existing modify clause inheritance behavior.

## Error Semantics

**on_apply error**: the condition is **never activated** (it fired
before activation, so there is nothing to roll back). Side effects
from the partial on_apply execution (mutations, emits) are not undone —
they are fire-and-forget, consistent with hook error semantics. The
error propagates to the caller of `apply_condition()`.

**on_remove error**: the condition is **still removed** (removal must
succeed for state consistency). The error is propagated but does not
prevent removal. When multiple instances are being removed, remaining
on_remove blocks still execute.

## Implementation Sketch

### AST changes (ttrpg_ast)

Add two new variants to `ConditionClause`:
```rust
pub enum ConditionClause {
    Modify(ModifyClause),
    Suppress(SuppressClause),
    OnApply(LifecycleBlock),   // new
    OnRemove(LifecycleBlock),  // new
}

pub struct LifecycleBlock {
    pub body: Vec<Stmt>,
    pub span: Span,
}
```

### Parser changes (ttrpg_parser)

Inside condition body parsing, recognize `on_apply` and `on_remove`
keywords followed by a block. Enforce at-most-one of each (parser
error on duplicates).

### Checker changes (ttrpg_checker)

- Type-check lifecycle block bodies in a scope containing `bearer` +
  condition params.
- **Enforce restrictions**: error on `apply_condition`, `remove_condition`,
  `revoke(invocation)`, and `invocation()` calls inside lifecycle blocks.
  Allow `revoke` on groups (syntactically distinct: `revoke entity.Group`
  vs `revoke(expr)`).
- Validate inherited lifecycle blocks from `extends` ancestors.

### Interpreter changes (ttrpg_interp)

- **apply_condition path**: collect on_apply blocks (ancestor chain,
  DFS post-order), execute each BEFORE inserting the ActiveCondition.
  On success, insert and activate. On error, do not insert; propagate.
- **remove_condition path**: expand to per-instance iteration in
  `gained_at` order. For each instance: collect on_remove blocks
  (ancestor chain, DFS post-order), execute each, then remove the
  instance. Continue regardless of on_remove errors; propagate first
  error after all removals complete.
- **revoke(invocation) path**: expand bulk removal into per-instance
  removals following the same on_remove protocol above.
- Runtime flag `in_lifecycle_block: bool` on execution environment to
  enforce restrictions dynamically as a safety net.

## Test Plan

1. **Partial failure in on_apply**: verify that mutations/emits from a
   failing on_apply are preserved (fire-and-forget) but the condition
   is never activated.
2. **revoke path**: verify per-instance on_remove execution in
   deterministic `gained_at` order when `revoke(invocation)` removes
   multiple conditions.
3. **Nested emit from lifecycle block**: verify that a hook triggered
   by emit inside on_apply CAN call `apply_condition` (indirect bypass
   is allowed), confirming restrictions are direct-only.
4. **Inherited lifecycle**: verify ancestor on_apply/on_remove blocks
   execute in correct DFS post-order.
5. **Multi-match remove_condition**: verify on_remove fires per instance
   and all instances are removed even if one on_remove errors.
6. **invocation() in lifecycle block**: verify checker error.

## Deferred to Phase 2

- **on_tick**: requires resolving game_time vs turn-relative timing,
  tick frequency, and ordering across entities
- **Auto-expiry**: duration-based automatic condition removal
- **Condition state**: mutable fields on conditions (if a use case
  emerges that needs persistence between lifecycle blocks)
title: Design: condition lifecycle hooks (on_apply/on_remove) — Phase 1

- Move hp/max_hp into group CombatStats declared in 'OSRIC Combat' (or keep in OSRIC core with mutation actions)

- Update all access paths (reads and writes) across all OSRIC systems
- Run all OSRIC integration tests to confirm no breakage

description: Bug in crates/ttrpg_parser/src/expr.rs at parse_match_expr (lines 507-514). The parser chooses guard match solely when the token after match is LBrace. Because map literals are parsed from LBrace in parse_primary_expr (lines 313-340), a valid pattern-match scrutinee that starts with a map literal is misparsed. Repro: ttrpg_parser::parse_expr("match {\"k\": 1} { _ => 0 }") should parse as PatternMatch with a MapLit scrutinee, but is routed into parse_guard_match and then errors. This blocks brace-leading expressions as match scrutinees unless users add extra parentheses.
title: match misclassifies map-literal scrutinee as guard match

close_reason: Fixed in commit 313628b (Fix 4 REPL/CLI bugs)
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/completer.rs

Why this is a bug: In the do, call, enable, and disable branches, the current token is derived with rest.trim(), but replacement start is computed as pos - current.len(). When the cursor is after a trailing space (for example do Attack ), trim() drops that space and word_start shifts right by one (or more), so the replacement span no longer matches the actual token. Accepting a completion can replace the wrong substring and corrupt input (for example producing do AAttack).

Potential impact: Tab completion can modify valid user input into invalid commands, causing confusing behavior and failed command execution in common interactive workflows.

Suggested fix: Derive the active token without trimming trailing whitespace (for example use a last-token parser like last_word(rest)), or detect trailing whitespace and avoid suggesting/replacing the previous token; compute span start from the true token boundary in line_to_cursor.
title: Bug: Completion span misaligned after trailing space

close_reason: Implemented ordered enums with ordinal/from_ordinal builtins
description:
Enums have no inherent ordering. In 5e, Size (tiny < small < medium < large < huge < gargantuan) needs comparison:
    requires { target.size <= grappler.size + 1 }
This is impossible because Size is an enum without ordinal values. Workaround is != checks for specific variants, which doesn't generalize.

Options:
- Ordered enums: `enum Size ordered { tiny, small, medium, large, huge, gargantuan }`
- Enum-to-int builtin: `ordinal(Size.medium) -> 2`
- Comparable enums that support <, >, <=, >= based on declaration order
title: Language: ordered enums / enum-to-int conversion

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/types.rs

Why this is a bug: In parse_type, the "option" branch only handles option<T> and otherwise returns TypeExpr::Named("option") immediately. Unlike the generic fallback path, it never checks for .Ident to form TypeExpr::Qualified. This means valid qualified types such as option.CustomType (when use "Other" as option is present) are misparsed, leaving .CustomType unconsumed and causing downstream parse errors.

Potential impact: Programs that use option as a module alias cannot reference imported types via qualified syntax, producing parse failures in otherwise valid code.

Suggested fix: In the "option" case, keep option<T> handling, but for non-< cases fall through to the same named/qualified logic used by the default identifier branch (including .Ident handling) instead of returning Named("option") directly.
title: Bug: parse_type rejects qualified types with option alias

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call/actions.rs

Why this is a bug: In dispatch_action_method, bind_args is called with only fn_info.params and action_decl.params, excluding the receiver from the binding context. bind_args evaluates default expressions in a temporary scope containing already-bound parameters. For action defaults that reference the receiver (allowed by checker because receiver is in scope when defaults are type-checked), method-call syntax (entity.Action()) evaluates those defaults without receiver bound and raises an undefined-variable runtime error on valid programs.

Potential impact: Valid DSL programs fail at runtime when calling actions with omitted optional args whose defaults reference the receiver, but the equivalent function-style action call works. This is inconsistent behavior between call syntaxes and causes unexpected runtime failures.

Suggested fix: In dispatch_action_method, evaluate defaults in an effective parameter context that includes the receiver as a first bound slot (mirroring dispatch_action), or explicitly push a scope and bind the receiver name/value before calling bind_args so default expressions can resolve it.
title: Bug: Action method defaults cannot reference receiver

This inconsistent default is a code smell and can permit calls under missing or invalid scope context. Define one explicit no-context policy and cover it with tests.

description: In crates/ttrpg_interp/src/reference_state.rs:238-248, add_condition stores the condition under the provided entity key but does not enforce cond.bearer == entity. This allows corrupted state where a condition attached to entity A reports bearer B. Downstream logic uses condition.bearer for binding/evaluation (e.g. crates/ttrpg_interp/src/pipeline.rs:83 and crates/ttrpg_interp/src/event.rs:419), so modifiers/suppressions can run against the wrong entity. description:
Support method-call syntax for action invocation. Currently actions are called as functions with the receiver as the first argument: Attack(attacker, target, weapon). This adds a method-call form: attacker.Attack(target, weapon), which desugars to the function-call form.

## Motivation
- Natural readability: 'attacker.Attack(target, weapon)' reads as subject-verb-object
- Consistency with existing method syntax on collections, options, dice, strings
- Actions already have an explicit receiver via 'on receiver: Type' — method syntax is the natural complement
- PascalCase action names visually distinguish from lowercase builtin methods

## Design (spec updated in 03_canonical_grammar.ttrpg)
- Both forms are equivalent: method form desugars to function form
- Resolution order for obj.Name(args): enum constructor > module alias > action dispatch > builtin method > error
- Action methods checked before builtin methods (PascalCase vs lowercase avoids collision)
- Context restrictions still apply (action/reaction/hook resolve blocks only)
- Named arguments work in both forms

## Implementation plan

### Checker (check_expr.rs)
1. In check_method_call(), before the catch-all error arm, add an action lookup:
   - Use env.lookup_fn(method) to find a FnInfo with kind == FnKind::Action
   - Verify the action's receiver type is compatible with obj_ty
   - Check context restrictions (must be in action/reaction/hook resolve block)
   - Build effective_params WITHOUT receiver (obj already fills that role)
   - Type-check remaining args against action params
   - Return the action's return type (unit)
2. Alternatively, intercept in the FieldAccess branch of check_call() before calling check_method_call()

### Interpreter (call.rs)
1. In eval_method_call(), before the catch-all error arm, add action dispatch:
   - Check if method name is an action in the program
   - The object value must be Value::Entity
   - Construct the full argument list with object as receiver, then remaining args
   - Call dispatch_action() with the synthetic argument list
2. Alternatively, intercept in the FieldAccess branch of eval_call() before calling eval_method_call()

### Tests
- Basic method syntax: entity.Action(args) works
- Equivalence: both forms produce the same result
- Named args: entity.Action(target: x, weapon: y)
- Error: method syntax on non-entity type
- Error: action name not matching receiver type
- Error: calling in wrong context (derive, etc.)
- No collision with builtin methods (different naming conventions)
title: Method-call syntax for actions: entity.Action(args)

Spellcasting optional group on Character needs: slots_per_level map, memorised_spells list. Ref: PG p33-50, 123-125.

description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/compare.rs

Why this is a bug: match_pattern() always treats PatternKind::Ident(name) as an enum-variant pattern when name exists in type_env.variant_to_enums. The checker intentionally allows bare identifiers to be variable bindings in binding/destructure contexts (check_pattern_binding and check_pattern_inner(..., in_destructure=true, ...)), even when the identifier matches a known variant name. Runtime and checker semantics therefore diverge for valid programs.

Potential impact: Valid for/comprehension patterns and destructuring subpatterns can fail to match or fail to bind variables when the binding name collides with any enum variant name (for example: for red in xs, or Variant(red) where red should bind a field). This causes incorrect control-flow and missed matches at runtime.

Suggested fix: Carry pattern context into runtime matching (binding-context / in-destructure flags) or use checker-resolved variant metadata so only syntactic variant patterns are matched as variants. In binding contexts, PatternKind::Ident should always bind.
title: Bug: match_pattern treats binding idents as enum variants

description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call/collection_builtins.rs

Why this is a bug: eval_sum accumulates integer lists with int_sum += n instead of checked arithmetic. On large valid int inputs (for example [i64::MAX, 1]), this can panic in debug builds or wrap in release builds rather than returning a RuntimeError, unlike other arithmetic paths that use checked_add.

Potential impact: Valid programs can crash interpreter executions or produce silently incorrect wrapped totals when summing large integer lists.

Suggested fix: Replace unchecked integer accumulation with checked_add and return RuntimeError::with_span on overflow, matching existing arithmetic overflow behavior.
title: Bug: sum() can overflow int accumulation

The run command supports pipe mode (non-terminal stdin), but check -c has no equivalent. Either: (1) make 'check -c -' read from stdin, (2) make 'check' without arguments read from stdin when it's a pipe (like run does), or (3) both.

design:
# Charge / Set-vs-Charge + Round Tracking Design

## Overview

Full implementation of OSRIC 3.0 §1.6.2.1 (Charging Into Combat) and §1.6.2.10 (Set Weapon Against Charge), plus the general-purpose round/time tracking infrastructure needed to support it (and spell durations, memorization, etc.).

## Game Time Model

### Unified counter (segments as base unit)
A single monotonically increasing int representing game time in segments:
- 1 round = 10 segments
- 1 turn = 100 segments (10 rounds)
- 1 hour = 600 segments
- 1 day = 14,400 segments

Comparison and arithmetic are trivial int ops. Structured accessors (to_round, to_turn, etc.) provide readability.

### Surprise is off-clock
Surprise segments happen before the game clock starts. The surprise resolution code manages segment-scoped effects locally (knows exactly how many surprise segments there are). The game clock begins at round 1, segment 1 (counter value 0 or 1).

Effects that need to carry from surprise into combat get promoted to round-based durations by the surprise code.

### Duration enum (expanded)
```
enum Duration {
    end_of_round,
    segments(count: int),   // managed locally (surprise, casting), not via game clock
    rounds(count: int),
    turns(count: int),
    hours(count: int),
    days(count: int),
    indefinite
}
```

`segments(n)` exists as a variant but is handled by local code (surprise resolution, casting time tracking) rather than the game clock expiry system. All other timed variants map to segment counts for clock-based expiry.

### Duration to segments mapping
```
derive duration_to_segments(d: Duration) -> option<int> {
    match d {
        Duration.end_of_round => some(10),
        Duration.segments(n)  => some(n),       // only used by local code
        Duration.rounds(n)    => some(n * 10),
        Duration.turns(n)     => some(n * 100),
        Duration.hours(n)     => some(n * 600),
        Duration.days(n)      => some(n * 14400),
        Duration.indefinite   => none
    }
}
```

## Condition Expiry (DSL-side)

### Interpreter changes
- Add `applied_at: i64` field to `ActiveCondition` (Rust struct)
- Add `env.current_game_time: Option<i64>` to interpreter Env
- `apply_condition` stamps `applied_at` from `env.current_game_time`
- `to_value()` exposes `applied_at` as an int field to DSL code
- Host sets `env.current_game_time` before calling DSL functions

### DSL-side expiry function
```
function expire_conditions(game_time: int, combatants: list<Character>) {
    for c in combatants {
        for cond in conditions(c) {
            let limit = duration_to_segments(cond.duration)
            if limit.is_some() {
                let elapsed = game_time - cond.applied_at
                if elapsed >= limit.unwrap() {
                    remove_condition(c, cond)
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### Round resolution integration
```
function resolve_round(
    game_time: int,          // NEW: current game time in segments
    round_number: int,       // NEW: for fighter attack alternation, cooldowns
    initiative: InitiativeResult,
    actions: list<RoundAction>
) {
    emit RoundStart(round: round_number, game_time: game_time)
    // ... existing segment loop (for seg in 1..=10) ...
    emit RoundEnd(round: round_number, game_time: game_time)
    // Expire timed conditions at round end
    // (combatant list derived from actions)
}
```

## Charge Rules (OSRIC 3.0 §1.6.2.1)

### What's needed
1. Weapon length data: add `length` field to MeleeWeaponDef (from Table 1.4.2.3c)
2. Charging condition: strips DEX AC bonus, -1 AC penalty if no DEX bonus
3. Charge cooldown: once per 10 rounds, tracked via `last_charge_time` entity field
4. Longer weapon first strike: if defender has longer weapon, attacks before charger
5. Encumbrance restriction: max encumbrance cannot charge unless mounted
6. Mounted lance double: lance + warhorse doubles weapon damage (not STR/magic/spec)

### Charge-vs-Set interaction (resolved in ChargeAction branch of resolve_action)
When ChargeAction resolves:
1. Apply Charging condition (loses DEX AC)
2. Check if target declared SetAgainstCharge in same round
3. If set: defender strikes first with 2x damage
4. If no set but defender has longer weapon: defender strikes first (normal damage)
5. If charger survives: resolve charge attack with +2 to-hit
6. Record last_charge_time for cooldown tracking

### Set-vs-Charge (§1.6.2.10)
- Set weapon readied at segment 1 (already implemented)
- Cannot attack unless actually charged
- Valid weapons: spear, javelin, lance (dismounted), pole arm, trident (already implemented)
- Double damage on hit against charger
title: Charge and set-vs-charge rules are only partially implemented

close_reason: Fixed: check for RParen before parsing bindings, allowing empty binding lists
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/decl.rs

Why this is a bug: parse_suppress_clause() always calls parse_modify_bindings(), and parse_modify_bindings() unconditionally parses at least one binding. This makes suppress clauses with empty binding lists unparseable. Downstream code handles empty suppress bindings (interpreted as unconditional match for the event), so parser behavior is inconsistent with checker/runtime semantics.

Potential impact: Conditions cannot express unconditional suppression of an event, and otherwise valid suppress clauses fail during parsing.

Suggested fix: In parse_suppress_clause(), allow empty parentheses by checking for RParen before calling parse_modify_bindings().
title: Bug: Empty suppress binding list rejected

close_reason: Fixed in commit 313628b (Fix 4 REPL/CLI bugs)
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_cli/src/runner/inspect.rs

Why this is a bug: In cmd_inspect, the three-segment path branch (handle.Group.field) reads state first and, when the Group key is absent, falls back to reporting '<handle>.<Group> is not currently granted' unless the group is declared as required. For an undeclared group name (e.g. inspect hero.Foo.bar where Foo is not a group/field on the entity type), this produces an incorrect diagnostic instead of an unknown group/field error.

Potential impact: Users receive misleading errors for typos and invalid inspect paths, which obscures actual schema problems and makes debugging harder.

Suggested fix: In the handle.Group.field branch, validate group_name against the entity type schema first (lookup_optional_group) and return an unknown-group error when absent; only use granted/missing status messaging for declared groups.
title: Bug: inspect misreports unknown Group.field as not granted

close_reason: Fixed all 5 interpreter/checker name resolution divergences
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/dispatch.rs

Why this is a bug: eval_ident resolves identifiers in a different order and visibility scope than checker check_ident. Runtime checks enum types and module aliases before conditions, and module aliases are matched across all systems (system_aliases.values().any(...)) instead of current-system visibility. A program that type-checks (for example, a condition name that collides with an enum type or an alias in another system) can evaluate to a different runtime value (EnumNamespace/ModuleAlias instead of Condition).

Potential impact: Well-typed programs can execute incorrect branches, pass wrong values to condition APIs, or fail at runtime due to mis-bound identifiers. Behavior can change based on unrelated declarations in other namespaces/systems.

Suggested fix: Make runtime identifier resolution mirror checker semantics exactly: same precedence order as check_ident and visibility filtering by current system/module metadata (not global alias scan).
title: Bug: eval_ident name resolution diverges from checker

description:
File: crates/ttrpg_parser/src/resolve.rs

Why this is a bug: desugar_qualified_types claims to walk all type expressions, but desugar_decl_types only calls desugar_modify_stmts for modify clauses and never visits ModifyClause.target. Selector predicates in ModifyTarget::Selector can contain TypeExpr via SelectorPredicate::Returns and SelectorPredicate::HasParam { ty }. Qualified types in those positions remain as TypeExpr::Qualified, so checker code later reports qualified type requires module resolution instead of resolving valid aliases.

Potential impact: Valid programs using qualified types in modify selectors fail type checking with false errors; alias and type ownership validation is skipped for these type positions.

Suggested fix: Extend resolver traversal to desugar selector predicate types in ModifyClause.target (for both condition and option modify clauses), recursing through Returns and optional HasParam type expressions before checking.
title: Bug: Qualified types in modify selectors are not resolved

description: In crates/ttrpg_checker/src/scope.rs lines 98-101, bind replaces bindings[name] but does not clear any existing narrowed_groups entry for that same key (see lines 169-176). If a name is rebound in the same scope after a narrowing was recorded, the new binding inherits stale optional-group proofs from the previous binding. This can suppress required checker diagnostics for optional-group access and with constraints. Suggested fix: when binding a name, remove narrowed_groups[name], or migrate narrowing state to binding IDs.

close_reason: FALSE POSITIVE: length equality check + BTree ordering makes zip comparison correct for same-type collections
description: File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval.rs (lines 1831-1842). In value_eq, Set and Map branches compare zipped iterator order instead of matching semantically equal elements/keys. Because semantic equality here includes cross-representation matches (e.g. Value::None equals Value::Option(None), Int(1) equals Float(1.0)), two semantically equal collections can have different BTree iteration order and compare false. Example: Set {None, Int(1)} vs {Option(None), Int(1)}. This can make runtime equality checks in production control flow return incorrect results.
title: value_eq can report semantically equal Set and Map values as unequal

description:
Add support for tagging modify clauses within conditions, enabling two capabilities:

1. **Targeted suppression by category**: Conditions can suppress entire categories of modifiers rather than needing to know specific condition names. E.g., `suppress [#position](target: bearer)` removes all position-based penalties, `suppress [#fear](target: bearer)` removes all fear effects.

2. **Audit trail / provenance**: The REPL can show a breakdown of where numeric modifiers come from, grouped by tag. E.g., `breakdown attack_roll(target: Thog)` shows each modifier with its tag, source condition, and value.

**Syntax sketch:**
```
condition Prone on bearer: entity {
    modify [#attack_resolution](target: bearer) #position #penalty {
        attack_mod = attack_mod + 4
    }
}

condition FreedomOfMovement on bearer: entity {
    suppress [#position](target: bearer)
}
```

**Scope:**
- Parser: allow tags after modify clause bindings, before body
- AST: store tags on ModifyClause
- Checker: validate tags exist
- Interpreter: filter tagged modify clauses during suppress evaluation
- REPL: add breakdown command showing provenance by tag

**Not in scope:** Removing boolean params from resolve_melee_attack (that is better solved by decomposing mechanics into sub-functions). Also not in scope: bonus type stacking rules (keep the DSL system-agnostic).
design:
## Design: Tags on Modify Clauses

### Syntax

Tags on modify clauses — after bindings, before body (consistent with action/function tag placement):

```
condition Prone on bearer: entity {
    modify [#attack_resolution](target: bearer) #position #penalty {
        attack_mod = attack_mod + 4
    }
}
```

### Suppress-Modify via Selector

New suppress variant using `[selector]` syntax (disambiguated from event suppress by leading `[`):

```
condition FreedomOfMovement on bearer: entity {
    suppress [#position](target: bearer)
}
```

- Supports full selector predicates: `#tag`, `returns Type`, `has param`
- Bindings are optional — omitting them suppresses all matching modify clauses regardless of entity
- Self-suppression is allowed (universal application, user error if contradictory)

### Semantics

**Suppress-modify evaluation** (in collect_modifiers_owned):
1. Collect all active suppress-modify clauses from entities in scope
2. For each candidate modifier, check if any suppress-modify clause matches by (a) tag/selector overlap and (b) binding match
3. Filter out suppressed modifiers before running phase 1/phase 2

**Binding match**: `suppress [#position](target: bearer)` means suppress any modify clause tagged #position where a binding named `target` resolves to this condition's bearer.

**Ordering**: Gained-order applies. Later-gained suppress overrides earlier-gained modifiers. "Un-suppress" (suppress a suppress) is out of scope for v0.

### Provenance

- `Effect::ModifyApplied` extended with modify clause tags
- REPL `breakdown` command shows per-modifier provenance: condition → tags → param/result changes → values
- Suppressed modifiers shown annotated (not omitted) for debugging

### AST Changes

- `ModifyClause`: add `tags: Vec<Name>`
- New `ConditionClause::SuppressModify` variant (separate from event suppress for clarity)

### Cost-modify tags deferred

Tags on `modify X.cost(...)` clauses are out of scope — separate concern with own binding semantics.

### Implementation Phases

1. AST: Add `tags: Vec<Name>` to `ModifyClause`
2. Parser: Parse `#tag` after modify bindings, before `{`
3. Checker: Validate modify clause tags exist
4. Interpreter: Include tags in `Effect::ModifyApplied`
5. AST: New `SuppressModify` variant
6. Parser: Parse `suppress [selector](bindings)`
7. Checker: Validate suppress-modify clauses
8. Interpreter: Filter suppressed modifiers in `collect_modifiers_owned`
9. REPL: `breakdown` command
10. Docs: Update spec and documentation
title: Add tags on modify clauses for provenance and targeted suppression

description: During the conditions/modify spec audit (tdsl-c92x), we found that ActiveCondition is only available as an internal Ty::ActiveCondition in the checker — users cannot reference it by name in DSL source code (e.g., as a parameter type in a user-defined event). This blocks user-defined override of modify_applied with the correct parameter type. Fix: add ActiveCondition to the type resolution path (either as a TypeExpr variant or a Named type that resolves to Ty::ActiveCondition).

description:
In crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_stmt.rs, the direct assignment branch in check_assign (lines 96-108) only checks mutable binding and does not forbid assigning to the implicit turn binding. Because turn is mutable, turn = ... type-checks. This conflicts with runtime behavior, which rejects direct turn reassignment and only permits field mutation (for example turn.actions -= 1). The mismatch causes a late runtime error instead of a checker error.

File references:
- crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_stmt.rs:96-108
- crates/ttrpg_checker/src/check_stmt.rs:147-149
title: Checker allows direct turn reassignment that runtime rejects

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/helpers.rs

Why this is a bug: resolve_resource_bounds only passes a one-segment prefix for group fields (or no prefix for normal fields) into eval_bound_expr. When a resource type is nested in a named struct (for example stats.hp: resource(0..=max_hp)), identifier lookup in the bound expression resolves max_hp as a top-level entity field instead of stats.max_hp (or Group.stats.max_hp). That causes bound evaluation to return None and disables clamping for valid nested resource declarations.

Potential impact: Assignments to nested resource fields can bypass min/max enforcement and write invalid values (for example negative HP) whenever bounds reference sibling fields in the same nested struct.

Suggested fix: Track and pass the full container path prefix to eval_bound_expr (all consumed FieldPathSegment::Field segments leading to the resource container), not just the optional-group root segment. Use that full prefix when resolving identifier names from resource bound expressions.
title: Bug: Nested struct resource bounds use wrong lookup prefix

close_reason: Implemented multi-class data model: ClassLevel struct, ClassingMode enum, helper/aggregation derives, all tests passing
description: Character currently has class: Class (single) and level: int (single). Multi-classing needs 2-3 simultaneous classes, dual-classing needs old+new class with level tracking. Proposed: change to classes: map<Class, int> mapping class to level. All derives that use class/level (bthb, saving_throws_for, xp_for_level, fighter_attacks_per_round, spell slots, etc.) must iterate over all held classes and pick best or aggregate. Consider: primary_class field for XP allocation, active_class for dual-classing restrictions, HP calculation (roll per class, divide by num classes). This touches osric_core, osric_class, osric_combat, osric_saves, osric_character, osric_initiative.
design:
## Data Model

### New types (osric_core.ttrpg)

```
struct ClassLevel {
    class: Class
    level: int
    xp: int
}

enum ClassingMode { Single, Multi, Dual }
```

### Character entity changes

Remove:
- `class: Class`
- `level: int = 1`
- `xp: int = 0`

Add:
- `classes: list<ClassLevel>`
- `classing_mode: ClassingMode`

### Accessor conventions

- `first(char.classes).unwrap()` — primary/only class (Single), or first class (Multi/Dual original)
- `last(char.classes).unwrap()` — current class for Dual, same as first for Single
- `max([...])` / iteration — best-of aggregation for BTHB, saves

### Rule divergence by mode

| Mechanic | Single | Multi | Dual |
|---|---|---|---|
| HP | class HD | averaged across classes | summed |
| BTHB | class table | best across classes | best across classes |
| Saves | class table | best per category | best per category |
| Armour | class permission | most restrictive | current class only (while dormant) |
| XP gain | all to class | split evenly | all to current class |
| Abilities | all active | all active | old dormant until new level > old level |

### Dual-class dormancy

Derived, not stored: `classes[1].level <= classes[0].level` means original class is dormant. No extra field needed.

### Files requiring changes

1. **osric_core** — entity + new types
2. **osric_combat** — `effective_target_ac` Monk checks, `resolve_melee/missile_attack` BTHB + Monk damage (use best_bthb, check any class == Monk)
3. **osric_saves** — `saving_throws_for` → aggregate best per category
4. **osric_character** — `prime_req_xp_bonus` → per-class or primary class
5. **osric_class** — `check_level_up` → per ClassLevel entry
6. **osric_initiative** — `has_fighter_multi_attack` → check any fighter-group class

### New aggregation derives needed

- `best_bthb(char) -> int` — max BTHB across all classes
- `best_saves(char) -> SavingThrows` — best per save category
- `effective_armour_permission(char) -> ArmourPermission` — mode-dependent
- `is_class(char, class) -> bool` — true if any ClassLevel matches (replaces `char.class == X`)
- `class_level(char, class) -> option<int>` — level for a specific class if held
- `has_active_class(char, class) -> bool` — like is_class but respects dual-class dormancy
- `primary_class(char) -> ClassLevel` — first entry (original/only)
- `current_class(char) -> ClassLevel` — last entry (active for dual, same as primary for single/multi)
title: Design: multi-class data model for Character entity

close_reason: Fixed all 4 alias/shadow resolution bugs
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_checker/src/env.rs

Why this is a bug: resolve_cost_token applies legacy aliases (e.g., action -> actions) before checking exact field-name matches. If a ruleset defines both singular and plural fields (for example, action and actions), cost token 'action' resolves to the alias target 'actions', making the exact 'action' field unreachable in cost clauses.

Potential impact: Valid schemas with both singular/plural resource names can have costs bound to the wrong field, causing incorrect type checking and runtime behavior.

Suggested fix: Prefer exact field-name matches before applying legacy alias remapping, or only apply aliases when no exact field exists.
title: Bug: Cost token alias resolution can shadow exact field names

close_reason: Fixed all 5 interpreter/checker name resolution divergences
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/eval/access.rs

Why this is a bug: In Value::ModuleAlias field access, variant resolution checks resolved_variants by expr.span and then falls back to unique_variant_owner(field). The checker alias path (resolve_alias_field) determines the target enum within the aliased system, but does not populate resolved_variants for this span. For valid code like Alias.Variant where the variant is unique in the aliased system but duplicated in another system, runtime lookup falls through and raises an error even though type checking succeeded.

Potential impact: Valid module-alias expressions can fail at runtime with no type, variant, or condition ... in module alias, breaking cross-system enum access when variant names overlap across systems.

Suggested fix: Persist alias-field variant resolution from the checker (for example insert into resolved_variants for the field-access span), or in runtime ModuleAlias handling resolve variants using alias target metadata instead of global unique_variant_owner.
title: Bug: Alias-qualified enum variant lookup ignores checker resolution

close_reason: Closed
description:
File: crates/ttrpg_interp/src/call/methods.rs

Why this is a bug: In eval_list_method, the sum() path adds integers with int_sum += n without checked arithmetic. For large valid int lists this can overflow i64. In debug builds this panics; in release it can wrap to incorrect values, violating the interpreters overflow error semantics used by other integer arithmetic operations.

Potential impact: Calling .sum() on large integer lists can crash the interpreter or return silently incorrect totals.

Suggested fix: Replace unchecked integer addition with checked_add and return RuntimeError when overflow occurs, consistent with other numeric operations.
title: Bug: list.sum() uses unchecked i64 accumulation
